did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a pre-trained ResNet-50 convolutional neural network (a deep learning model) to analyze uploaded images of skin lesions and classify them as either melanoma or allergy, achieving 97.23% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a Streamlit-based application with two modules (Common Diseases Checker and Skin Disease Classifier). It details the methodology using specific machine learning models (Decision Tree, KNN, ResNet-50), reports quantitative results (accuracy metrics like 97.23% and 98.87%), and includes a workflow and interface analysis. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICAIT-2024), it contains original technical contributions and empirical validation rather than merely summarizing prior literature.

title: Revolutionizing Healthcare Triage: A Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning-Driven Symptom Checkers and Triage Bots for Common Diseases and Skin Conditions
journal: 2024 Second International Conference on Advances in Information Technology (ICAIT)
brief_summary: This study developed a Streamlit-based healthcare triage application featuring two modules: a Common Diseases Checker and a Skin Disease Classifier. The Common Diseases Checker utilized a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) model to predict conditions based on user-reported symptoms, achieving 98.87% accuracy. The Skin Disease Classifier employed a pre-trained ResNet-50 convolutional neural network to analyze uploaded images of skin lesions, classifying them as melanoma or allergy with 97.23% accuracy. The system was evaluated against other baseline models, including Decision Trees and Random Forests, demonstrating superior performance for the respective tasks. The application aims to improve healthcare accessibility by providing remote, user-friendly diagnostic support for common ailments and skin conditions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classes in the Skin Disease Classifier module, trained using a pre-trained ResNet-50 model on a dataset of skin lesion images.
Used as the second binary class in the Skin Disease Classifier module, representing non-melanoma skin conditions in the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Disease labels for common diseases and skin lesion categories (melanoma vs. allergy)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes two classification tasks: 1) Common Disease Checker using a Decision Tree classifier to predict potential health conditions based on user-provided symptoms (binary severity assessment: severe vs. negligible/minor). 2) Skin Disease Classifier using a pre-trained ResNet-50 model to perform binary classification of skin lesion images into 'melanoma' or 'allergy' classes, achieving 97.23% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: Common Disease Symptom Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing symptom descriptions, severities, precautions, and disease labels used for training the Common Diseases Checker module. The specific source or name of the public/private dataset is not reported in the text.

use.training: Used to train a Decision Tree classifier and other baseline models (Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, KNN) for predicting potential health conditions based on user-provided symptoms.
use.testing: Used to evaluate model accuracy (e.g., Decision Tree achieved 99.49%, KNN achieved 98.87%).

datasets_used.name: Skin Lesion Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of skin lesion images with corresponding labels (melanoma or allergy) used for training the Skin Disease Classifier module. The specific source or name of the public/private dataset is not reported in the text.

use.training: Used to fine-tune a pre-trained ResNet-50 model for binary classification of skin lesions. Also used to evaluate baseline models (ResNet18, DenseNet, Standard CNN).
use.testing: Used to evaluate prediction accuracy (ResNet-50 achieved 97.23%), sensitivity, and specificity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and usability metrics without statistical significance testing.

Common Disease Symptom Dataset.notes: The Decision Tree classifier achieved the highest accuracy (99.49%) for common disease prediction. KNN was selected as the primary model for the application, achieving 98.87% accuracy. The paper also mentions evaluating precision, recall, and usability for this module, but specific values are not reported.

Skin Lesion Image Dataset.notes: ResNet50 outperformed other baseline models (ResNet18, DenseNet, Standard CNN) with an accuracy of 97.23%. The paper also mentions evaluating sensitivity, specificity, and usability for this module, but specific values are not reported.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained (fine-tuned for binary classification of skin lesions)

architecture_details.classifier_head: Binary classification head for melanoma vs. allergy

training_details.dataset: Skin lesion image dataset with labels (melanoma or allergy)

conclusions: The developed Streamlit-based application successfully integrates a Common Diseases Checker using a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) model and a Skin Disease Classifier using a pre-trained ResNet-50 model to provide remote healthcare triage.

The KNN model achieved 98.87% accuracy in predicting common diseases based on user-reported symptoms, outperforming baseline models such as Logistic Regression (91.76%), Support Vector Machine (93.08%), Decision Tree (99.49% was highest but KNN was selected for the application), and Random Forest (98.91%).

The ResNet-50 model achieved 97.23% accuracy in classifying skin lesions as either melanoma or allergy, significantly outperforming baseline models including ResNet18 (67.64%), DenseNet (28.89%), and Standard CNN (64.72%).

The system addresses healthcare accessibility challenges by providing a user-friendly interface that bridges gaps in healthcare communication, particularly for underserved regions, by offering early diagnosis and precautionary measures.
The application demonstrates the potential of combining natural language processing and computer vision to democratize healthcare services and empower users with proactive health management tools.

Limitations acknowledged include potential diagnostic inaccuracies for complex diseases and privacy concerns regarding the storage of sensitive medical data, necessitating ongoing refinement of the system's design and functionality.

Future work includes integrating telemedicine capabilities (such as video conferencing and appointment scheduling) and leveraging data from wearable devices to enhance personalized health insights and early detection.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges limitations regarding potential diagnostic inaccuracies for complex diseases and privacy concerns related to the storage of sensitive medical data.

The specific source, size, and demographic composition of the datasets used for both common disease symptoms and skin lesion images are not reported, raising concerns about dataset bias and reproducibility.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on the internal training/testing splits of the unspecified datasets.
Formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were not reported; performance comparisons rely exclusively on accuracy metrics.

The Skin Disease Classifier performs a binary classification task (melanoma vs. allergy), which is a simplified clinical scenario that does not reflect the complexity of real-world dermatological differential diagnoses.
The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, precision, or recall values for the final deployed models (KNN and ResNet-50), only accuracy, limiting the assessment of clinical utility.
The application is a proof-of-concept prototype built with Streamlit and lacks integration with clinical workflows, electronic health records, or telemedicine capabilities at the time of publication.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; the methodology describes splitting data but does not detail how temporal or patient-level leakage was prevented during the split.
